macro_segments.title: Introduction to Shchiv Mera Transactions and the Concept of Chelek (Portion)

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and introduction to Daf Samech Gimmel
Definition and framework of shchiv mera (deathbed declaration) and conditional transactions
The distinction between yachlok (divide equally) and tnu chelek (give a portion) in ambiguous language
Ravina bar Kissi's citation of a braita within Sumchos's position regarding the meaning of chelek

macro_segments.title: Sumchos's Framework: How Chelek Scales Based on Context (Water Rights)

micro_segments.title: Chelek in a bor (cistern): minimum is a tiny bit plus half the disputed amount, totaling a quarter
Chelek in a chavit (barrel): smaller quantity, resulting in one-eighth plus a little bit
Chelek in a kdeira (cooking pot) and tafiach (drinking glass): progressively smaller allocations
Discussion of water usage patterns and practical applications in agricultural and domestic contexts

macro_segments.title: Ben Levi's Field: Retaining Rights to Terumah and the Problem of Davar Shelo Ba L'Olam

micro_segments.title: Case of a Levi selling a field to an Israelite while retaining rights to the first tithe (maaser rishon)
The dilemma: intangible rights versus property ownership and the principle of davar shelo ba l'olam
Resolution: the Levi retains an abstract legal right (zechut) to be the designated recipient, not property ownership
Variations: retaining rights for heirs, conditional on the field remaining in the buyer's possession, and the limits of transferability

macro_segments.title: Reish Lakish's Principle: Selling a House with Retained Rights to the Upper Story

micro_segments.title: Reish Lakish's case: when a seller explicitly retains diyuta elyona (upper course of bricks) rights via al menat condition

macro_segments.title: Reish Lakish's Principle: Selling a House with Retained Rights to the Upper Story (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rav Zevid's interpretation: the seller gains the right to build zizin (ledges) on the upper structure
Rav Pappa's expansion: the seller can even build an entire additional story (aliyah) on top
Difficulty with Rav Pappa's position: whether it truly fits the language of Reish Lakish's zos omeres implication

macro_segments.title: Rav Dimi's Doctrine: The Necessity of Explicit Language for Umka V'Ruma (Depths and Heights)

micro_segments.title: Rav Dimi's position: writing umka v'ruma alone does not convey rights; must explicitly write from tehom ara to rum rakia
The reasoning: umka v'ruma in isolation conveys nothing; must reference the full range from depths to heavens
Potential proof from the next mishna regarding bor, dut, and michilot (cisterns, tanks, and tunnels)
Reinterpretation of the mishna: even without writing umka v'ruma, it functions as if written, conferring rights to bor, dut, and michilot

macro_segments.title: The Roof with Maakaeh: Implicit Rights and the Role of Physical Structure in Property Transfer

micro_segments.title: Case from mishna: when a house is sold with a maakaeh (parapet) ten tefachim high, the roof is retained by the seller
The logic: a high maakaeh makes the roof a separate usable area (chashiv), not automatically transferred in a stam (simple) sale
Implication from Reish Lakish: the necessity of al menat (on condition) language when seller wants to retain roof construction rights
Ravina to Rav Ashi: challenging the interpretation—what does al menat actually mean if stam (simple) already excludes air rights?
Alternative interpretation: al menat creates a condition of liability—if the upper course collapses, the seller must rebuild
Rashbam's dissatisfaction: multiple interpretations exist, none fully satisfactory; issue remains unresolved
